content_en:
Wafayat al-A'yan wa Anba' Abna' az-Zaman — whose title translates roughly as "Deaths of Eminent Men and News of the Sons of the Age" — is organized alphabetically rather than by generation or chronology. This alphabetical arrangement was not unprecedented in Arabic biographical literature, but Ibn Khallikan applied it with exceptional consistency and rigor, and the work became the model for later Arabic biographical dictionaries organized on this principle.

The alphabetical arrangement serves a clear practical purpose: it makes the work a reference tool that can be consulted efficiently by anyone looking for a specific individual. Unlike tabaqat works organized by generation, which reward sequential reading to understand the development of a tradition over time, the Wafayat is designed for targeted lookup. This represents a shift in the conception of biographical literature from a narrative history to a reference database — a shift that reflects the needs of a scholarly community that was using such works for practical information retrieval.

Ibn Khallikan's criteria for inclusion are also distinctive and worth examining. He explicitly states that he includes only individuals who are famous enough that educated people are expected to know who they are — the test is not scholarly importance in any narrow sense but general cultural prominence. This means the Wafayat covers not just jurists and hadith scholars but poets, physicians, mathematicians, musicians, governors, generals, and other figures who made a mark on Islamic civilization. This broad cultural scope distinguishes it sharply from school-specific biographical dictionaries.

For each entry, Ibn Khallikan follows a general pattern: full name and genealogy, birth date and place, account of the subject's life and achievements, selection of notable sayings or literary productions, date and place of death, and often an etymological or historical note on the subject's name or nisba (regional designation). The literary quality of the entries varies but is consistently high — Ibn Khallikan was himself a man of letters as well as a jurist, and his entries often contain elegant observations and well-chosen anecdotes.

Critical evaluation of sources is a persistent feature of the methodology. Ibn Khallikan regularly notes where his sources disagree, records multiple versions of disputed facts, and explicitly acknowledges uncertainty when his information is incomplete. This scholarly conscientiousness sets the work apart from biographical literature that presents uncertain information with false confidence.

The work excludes the Prophet's Companions (Sahabah) by design — Ibn Khallikan notes that this subject already has extensive dedicated literature. He also excludes the Four Caliphs and other figures from the very earliest Islamic period who are covered exhaustively elsewhere. His coverage begins effectively with the generation after the Companions and extends to his own contemporaries.
